lines.text: Chapter one, section one. Overview and Objectives.
Welcome to the first chapter of our companion to Play Optimal Poker by Andrew Brokos.
Before we ever look at a poker hand, we need to build our understanding of one of the most important ideas in all of game theory: the concept of equilibrium.

Brokos dedicates this entire opening chapter to it, and for good reason — if you don't have a firm grip on what equilibrium means and how to reason about it, everything that follows will be much harder to absorb.

So this chapter takes a deliberate detour away from poker itself and instead examines a handful of extremely simple, everyday situations — situations you have almost certainly been in before, but probably never thought of as exercises in game theory.

The approach here is intentional: by starting with scenarios that are simple enough to reason through completely, you can focus entirely on the logic of equilibrium without getting distracted by the complexity of cards, bet sizes, and hand ranges.
That groundwork then transfers directly to poker once we're ready to go there.

Along the way, the chapter also introduces two other foundational game-theory ideas that will appear constantly throughout the rest of the book: the precise meaning of the word 'strategy' as game theorists use it, and the role that computers play in solving equilibria for games that are too complicated for human reasoning alone.
By the time you finish this chapter, you should be able to clearly define both 'strategy' and 'equilibrium' the way game theorists define them — not loosely, but with real precision.
You should be able to look at a pair of strategies and determine whether they are in equilibrium or not, and to explain exactly why you reached that conclusion.

You should understand the difference between a pure strategy — always doing the same thing — and a mixed strategy, where a player randomizes among options, and you should understand when each type is necessary.
And perhaps most practically, you should understand what it actually takes to randomize your play in a way that is genuinely unpredictable and therefore unexploitable.
That last point might sound simple, but as we will see later in the chapter, it turns out to be surprisingly difficult for human beings to do correctly.
Let's start by getting the vocabulary right.
